title: Contact Testing Overview
narrationScript: Contact testing is the method you will reach for most often, so let's understand it from the ground up. The name says it plainly. The probe sits in direct contact with the part, separated only by a thin film of couplant, and your hand drives it across the surface. No tank, no water column, no robot. Just the probe, a smear of couplant, and a steady hand. That simplicity is exactly why contact testing dominates field work. You can carry the gear in one case, set up in minutes, and reach a weld on a pipe rack forty feet in the air. Start with why couplant exists at all. Sound is mechanical vibration, and it has to physically pass from the probe face into the steel. But a probe pressed against bare metal still traps a microscopic layer of air between them, and the boundary between steel and air reflects almost one hundred percent of the sound straight back. Almost nothing crosses. That air gap is a wall. Couplant fills it. A liquid or gel with an acoustic impedance between air and the steel lets the energy bridge across instead of bouncing off. Common choices are water, glycerin, cellulose gels, and various oils and pastes, and for hot surfaces there are special high temperature couplants rated for the heat. The couplant is not optional and it is not a lubricant. It is part of the sound path, and a dry spot means no signal. Now pressure and motion. A beginner squeezes the probe hard, thinking more force means more sound. It does not. Once you have a continuous couplant film and solid contact, extra force adds almost nothing and only tires your hand and wears the wedge. What matters is consistency. Keep the coupling steady, the probe flat against the surface, and the couplant film even, so the signal does not jump around as you scan. Move at a controlled speed, slow enough that an indication cannot slip between sweeps, and overlap each pass so you cover the whole volume. On a curved surface like a pipe, a flat probe rocks and loses contact at the edges, so you either use a contoured wedge ground to the radius or you keep the probe aligned along the axis where the surface is flattest. Surface condition makes or breaks a contact test. Loose scale, weld spatter, rust, and heavy paint scatter the sound and starve the signal, so you clean and sometimes lightly grind the scan area first. A rough surface also wears probe faces quickly and traps couplant unevenly. Let's talk about what contact testing does well and where it struggles. Its strengths are speed, portability, single sided access, and immediate answers, which is why it owns weld inspection, corrosion thickness surveys, lamination checks in plate, and bond testing in the field. Straight beam contact probes measure thickness and find laminations. Angle beam contact probes on wedges inspect welds. Dual element contact probes shine on corroded and thin material. Its weaknesses follow from the human hand. Coupling varies as you move, so amplitude is less repeatable than an automated method. The near surface dead zone, that blind region right under the initial pulse, can hide shallow flaws. And because results depend on your technique, two technicians can get different readings on the same part. The fix is discipline. Calibrate on a reference block in the same condition as the part, apply transfer correction when the part surface is rougher than the block, keep your couplant and speed consistent, and scan a deliberate overlapping pattern every time. Contact testing rewards a steady hand and punishes a careless one. Master the basics here and most of the field work in this trade is within reach.
imagePrompt: A technician in coveralls presses an angle-beam transducer on a clear-plastic wedge against a freshly ground pipe weld, a smear of couplant glistening at the contact line, a portable flaw detector showing a live A-scan resting on the pipe beside his hand.

sections.heading: What Contact Testing Is
sections.body:
In **contact testing** the transducer is placed directly on the test surface, separated only by a thin film of **couplant**, and the inspector moves it by hand. There is no water tank, no standoff column, and no mechanized carriage in the basic method — just the probe, a layer of couplant, and the part.

That simplicity is the whole reason contact testing is the **workhorse of field UT**. The equipment fits in a single case, setup takes minutes, and the technique reaches places no tank ever could: a weld on an elevated pipe rack, the shell of an in-service vessel, a structural connection in a building frame.

The basic contact families you will use are:

- **Straight (normal) beam** — longitudinal waves straight into the part for thickness gauging and lamination detection.
- **Angle beam on a wedge** — shear waves injected at 45°, 60°, or 70° for weld examination.
- **Dual element** — separate transmit and receive crystals in one housing, excellent on **thin and corroded** material and near-surface work.
- **Delay line** — a short plastic standoff that moves the dead zone out of the part for thin-section precision thickness.

sections.heading: Why Couplant Is Part of the Sound Path
sections.body:
Ultrasound is mechanical vibration, so the energy must physically cross from the probe face into the metal. The problem is the interface. Even a probe pressed firmly on clean steel traps a microscopically thin layer of **air**, and the **steel-to-air boundary reflects nearly 100% of the sound** straight back — almost nothing crosses. That trapped air is an acoustic wall.

**Couplant** removes the air. A liquid or gel whose acoustic impedance sits between air and the steel lets the energy bridge the gap instead of bouncing off it. This is the same impedance-mismatch physics that makes a flaw visible — we exploit it at the flaw and defeat it at the front surface.

Common couplants include **water, glycerin, cellulose-based gels, oils, and proprietary pastes**; **high-temperature couplants** are formulated for hot surfaces where ordinary gels boil or burn off. The couplant is **not a lubricant and not optional** — it is part of the acoustic path. A dry spot means no signal reaches the part, so the entire scan zone must stay wetted.

sections.caption: A contact setup: the probe, the thin couplant film bridging the steel-to-air gap, the part, and the sound path down to a back-wall echo on the A-scan.

sections.heading: Probe Handling and Scanning Pressure
sections.body:
A common beginner mistake is to **press hard**, assuming more force pushes in more sound. It does not. Once you have a **continuous couplant film and solid, flat contact**, additional pressure adds almost no signal — it only fatigues your hand and wears the wedge. What the signal actually needs is **consistency**.

Good contact technique comes down to a few habits:

- Keep the probe **flat** against the surface so the full face couples and the beam enters at the intended angle.
- Hold a **steady, even couplant film** so amplitude does not jump as you move.
- Scan at a **controlled speed** — slow enough that no indication can slip between sweeps.
- **Overlap each pass** (commonly by at least 10% of the transducer width) so the whole volume is covered.

On **curved surfaces** such as pipe, a flat probe rocks and loses contact at the edges. Either use a **contoured wedge** ground to the radius, or orient the probe along the **axis** of the pipe where the surface is flattest. Rocking changes both the coupling and the refracted angle, which corrupts both amplitude and flaw location.

sections.title: Coupling consistency beats coupling force
sections.body: Beyond a continuous couplant film and flat, solid contact, **squeezing harder does not increase signal** — it just wears the wedge and tires your hand. The goal is a **steady, even film** and a controlled, overlapping scan so amplitude stays repeatable. A dry spot is a blind spot: keep the entire scan zone wetted.

sections.heading: Surface Condition and Couplant Choice
sections.body:
Surface condition makes or breaks a contact test, because everything must pass through that thin interface. **Loose scale, weld spatter, rust, and heavy paint** scatter sound and starve the signal, so the scan area is cleaned — and sometimes lightly ground — before inspection. A rough surface also **wears probe faces** quickly and traps couplant unevenly, causing amplitude to wander.

Couplant is matched to the job:

- **Smooth, cool surfaces:** light gels or oils spread thin and clean up easily.
- **Rough or vertical surfaces:** thicker pastes and gels stay put and fill the texture.
- **Hot surfaces:** **high-temperature couplants** rated for the metal temperature; ordinary couplant boils off and the velocity in the part also drops with temperature, so the instrument is recalibrated for hot work.
- **Sensitive parts:** a couplant compatible with the material and any later process (some parts forbid oils or sulfur/halogen-bearing couplants).

The practical point: a smoother surface and the right couplant give a **stronger, steadier, more repeatable** signal — which is why surface prep is the first real step of most contact examinations.

sections.caption: Where contact testing fits among the basic methods — strengths and the limits to manage.
rows: Thin couplant film, probe moved by hand
Field welds, corrosion thickness, laminations, bond checks
Fast — minutes, portable in one case
Coupling varies by hand; near-surface dead zone; operator-dependent

sections.heading: Strengths, Limits, and the Discipline That Fixes Them
sections.body:
Contact testing's **strengths** flow from its simplicity: speed, portability, single-side access, low cost, and an immediate answer on the screen. That is why it owns **weld inspection, corrosion and thickness surveys, lamination checks, and field bond testing**.

Its **limits** flow from the human hand:

- **Coupling varies** as you scan, so amplitude is less repeatable than an automated method — this is the biggest practical weakness.
- The **dead zone** just beneath the initial pulse can mask very shallow, near-surface flaws (a dual-element or delay-line probe helps here).
- Results depend on **operator skill**, so two technicians can read the same part differently.

The defenses are pure discipline: **calibrate on a reference block** in the same surface condition as the part; apply **transfer correction** when the part is rougher than the block; hold couplant and scan speed **consistent**; and run a **deliberate, overlapping scan pattern** every time. Contact testing rewards a steady, methodical hand and punishes a careless one — the technique is simple, but doing it repeatably is the skill this course builds.

figures.title: Contact test setup with couplant layer and sound path

checkQuestions.question: Why is couplant essential in contact ultrasonic testing?
options: It lubricates the probe so it slides more easily
A trapped air layer reflects nearly all the sound, and couplant bridges that gap so energy enters the part
It increases the frequency of the probe
It cools the transducer during long scans
checkQuestions.explanation: A steel-to-air interface reflects close to 100% of the sound; couplant fills the air gap with a medium of intermediate impedance so energy crosses into the part. It is part of the sound path, not a lubricant.

checkQuestions.question: An inspector finds the echo amplitude jumps around as he scans a weld. The most likely cause is:
options: The probe frequency is drifting
Inconsistent coupling — an uneven or breaking couplant film and rocking of the probe
The back wall is too far away
The gain is set too low
checkQuestions.explanation: In contact testing the hand controls coupling; an uneven film, dry spots, or rocking on a curved surface make amplitude wander. Consistent flat contact and an even film keep the signal steady.

checkQuestions.question: Once a continuous couplant film and flat, solid contact are achieved, pressing the probe harder against the part will:
options: Roughly double the signal amplitude
Add almost no signal and mainly wear the wedge and tire the hand
Shift the refracted angle to a steeper value
Eliminate the dead zone
checkQuestions.explanation: Beyond solid contact and a continuous film, extra force adds little signal. Coupling consistency, not force, is what keeps amplitude repeatable.

checkQuestions.question: Which application is contact testing especially well suited to?
options: High-speed automated production scanning of thousands of identical parts
Field examination of a weld on an elevated, in-service pipe from one side
Inspecting parts with extremely complex internal geometry that need a focused water-coupled beam
Mapping a full C-scan image of a large composite panel in one pass
checkQuestions.explanation: Contact testing is portable, single-sided, and fast to set up, making it ideal for field welds and in-service inspection. Automated production scanning and focused C-scan imaging are better suited to immersion methods.

checkQuestions.question: Before a contact examination, a heavily scaled and rusty surface is cleaned and sometimes lightly ground primarily because:
options: Rust changes the chemical composition of the steel
Scale and rust scatter sound and trap couplant unevenly, weakening and destabilizing the signal
A clean surface raises the frequency of the returning echoes
Grinding is required by every code regardless of surface state
checkQuestions.explanation: Loose scale, rust, and spatter scatter the beam and hold couplant unevenly, starving and destabilizing the signal; surface prep gives a stronger, steadier, more repeatable reading.

references: General practice: couplant defeats the steel-to-air impedance mismatch (R ≈ 100% at steel/air)
Codes such as ASME Section V, Article 4 (contact examination, surface preparation, and reference-block calibration)
